Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after pressing RIGHT! Let me check what specifically changed by comparing the before and after frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after RIGHT
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation

before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before_seg['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after_seg['nodes']}

print("Changes detected:")
for nid in after_nodes:
    if nid in before_nodes:
        bn = before_nodes[nid]
        an = after_nodes[nid]
        if bn['hash'] != an['hash'] or bn['pixels'] != an['pixels'] or bn['boundary'] != an['boundary']:
            print(f"  Node {nid} (color={an['color']}): pixels {bn['pixels']}->{an['pixels']}, hash {bn['hash']}->{an['hash']}")
            if bn['boundary'] != an['boundary']:
                print(f"    boundary changed")
    else:
        print(f"  New node {nid} (color={after_nodes[nid]['color']})")

for nid in before_nodes:
    if nid not in after_nodes:
        print(f"  Removed node {nid} (color={before_nodes[nid]['color']})")

# Also check the upper bar region specifically
print("\nUpper bar region (rows 17-22, cols 10-45):")
ascii_lines = after.ascii.split('\n')
for i in range(17, 23):
    print(f"  Line {i}: {ascii_lines[i][10:45]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  Node 6 (color=b): pixels 8->8, hash 9059581ee351aa20->1fd5a6753462fbd0

Upper bar region (rows 17-22, cols 10-45):
  Line 17: BBbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
  Line 18: BBbSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
  Line 19: BBbSSSSggggggggggggggggggggggSSbbBB
  Line 20: BBbSSSSggggggggggggggggggggggSSbbBB
  Line 21: BBbSSSSBBBBBBggggggggggggggggSSbbBB
  Line 22: BBbbbbbBBBBBBggggggggggggggggSSbbBB

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So pressing RIGHT swapped the colors of nodes 5 and 6 on the right end of the upper bar. Node 5 was sky blue (S) and node 6 was blue (b), and now they've swapped. Looking at the ASCII:
- Before: `...bbSSBB` (blue border, sky blue fill)
- After: `...SSbbBB` (sky blue fill, blue border)
So the right end of the bar had the blue border on the outside and sky blue on the inside, and now it's reversed. This looks like the bar is tracking some kind of state - maybe a "fill level" or "progress" that moves from left to right.
Let me understand the structure better:
- Left side: blue (b) border container with sky blue (S) interior - this looks "filled"
- Middle: gray (g) bar
- Right side: was blue border + sky blue interior, now sky blue + blue border
It seems like RIGHT moves something from left to right on this bar. Let me try pressing RIGHT more times to see the pattern, and also try LEFT to reverse it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The RIGHT action moved/swapped elements on the upper bar's right end
# Let me try pressing RIGHT again to see the pattern continue
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
